author: @stevecummins324
text: Where I work has a couple of custom machines made of such. The machines work great until after a while the t nuts work themselves loose due to aluminium squishing under vibration/ pressure. Then product slams into things like sensors or retracting stops, and snaps  them off.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - I have seen entire factories made of this stuff

author: @HeimoVN
text: We have a company locally where I live where you just create a list on their website of the sections you need, and they precisely cut those extrusions for you to length and you only pay for what you need, and no need to even cut it yourself...

author: @edwardcdg
text:
I was wondering about sourcing the multi-hole punch.
For the capital-challenged, aluminum C- or L-channel is cheaper and more widely available.  I have found it as scraps in a salvage yard.
Thanks again!

author: @scotttovey
text:
Nice one Rob.
If you ever need to combine a stack of paper into a book, all you need to do is add a fence to maintain the same depth of punch and punch away.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: are you going to use that for building layers for batteries or something?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: exactly

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
This building material is what made me seriously look into what it takes to set up an extruder for extruding aluminum period, as I would love to make some bigger stuff in an L shape with the slots and everything, (like extend that 1x2 piece you have out till it is not 2 rows of slots but 4 and then on one side have at least 3 if not 4 slots but the ends have only one slot on the edge and the other side would only have 2 or 3 slots inside the elbow shape, for the corners of a building, to make building a whole skyscraper like building as easy as you are finding at building that platform for your machine, 

one idea I had was to use round slugs of aluminum and make sure they are milled flat then when one is about pushed through the extruder, put in a new slug and put pressure but rotating it at the same time for a bit to weld it to the remnant of the last slug before forcing it all through the extruder, the other is to maybe find a way to use a wratcheting system to force everything through so regular hydralic press of 20 to 50 or even 100 tons is not necessary, so the ability to hold in the sides with that much pressure is not needed either, (or ultrasonic vibration while pressing forward sort of how air powered gravers work as well as reciprocating engravers or something LOL)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they do quite a range these days mate I have seen some pretty heavy duty machines made from this stuff

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah but the fact that aluminum is just loaded into a tube and pushed through a die like making noodles or something is what amazes me, and how the chunks of aluminum actually bond end to end due to all that squeezing and rearranging, and such. all to make the myriad of extruded aluminum stuff man, the possibilities. I saw some that were basically angle but had lips added for holding edges of panels and such, and that was kinda what I was thinking but with slots in it to allow so many customizable options outside just being a frame for a window and a ceiling/floor of the next level and what not. just seems a shame there is not a source but hey, if aluminum is so sticky and soft it should be possible to just make the stuff here, so I can always get more by making it since aluminum scrap is super easy to come by, and only takes what? 660ºC to melt? be really easy to make the ingots to push through it, though I also wonder if carbon nano tubes would withstand that and let you stir them into the aluminum so it would get organized in straight-sh lines when the aluminum is squeezed through the die making the extrusions super strong and slightly lighter even than they already are :)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: oh yeah if you still have a 3D printer you can make nylon t-nuts for those too, that are self locking and quite strong, and the nylon is plastic so if you strip it out you can heat it up and the plastic will melt solid again and rethread the nut again, just need to do 100% infill though you can also use solid stuff like delrin and pure nylon or polycarbonate (*the stuff I printed with is called Taulman 230 meaning it is a nylon/polycarbonate hybrid that has other stuff in it that makes it extrudable at 230 degrees or even a little less, and I found that kapton tape and nothing else not even heating of the bed was necessary for printing smaller parts from it, ) I think I still have a video on here for the nuts I made not sure.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate - cheers

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: That is the exact T-Track that I bought and I cut them in half with my band saw and made strong T-Track for my radial arm saw table. They work very well. Cheers mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - cheers

replies.author: @stuffandthings6188
replies.text: What was the t track on the radial arm saw for? I can only guess it was a length stop?

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: @stuffandthings6188 It was for a stop and for the hold down. See, I bought the router head and every router blade to go with the saw. It makes molding as well as saw. The track allows me to use the hold down wheels that where bought also and I can use feathers with it that I made myself with PVC board. Those t-tracks are also used as guides and to attach hold down clamps for cross-cuts.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: on the hole punch I wonder if less pressure could be used if you did a rolling hole punch where you had each punch in a sleeve like the hand held hole punches for sheet metal and a wheel that rolls down a row pushing each one in and through as it went with out the paper stack slipping, or not. seems it would lower the pressure needed like making a bead on sheet metal works far easier from a roller than trying to curl it all at once. and like me you can put a compound curling action to make stuff like my goggle rims where the bead can be curved around in a circle to hold a little ridge sticking out of a round lens :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh - I am sure there are hundred ways you could do this job ate

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: is that 80/20? LOL about time you did a video on that stuff XD (or is it V-slot since  there is a difference and there is some other company making something similar but I think most if not all of their stuff like V-slot brand for openbuilds, is all metric where as the 80/20 comes in both varieties series 10 is what I have all inch based 1x1 and 1x2 from zoro, 25 bucks per 8 foot 1 inch long sticks and 42 I think for the same length in 1020 (1x2 inch sized) with orders over 50 bucks shipped free, which is where the real expense is for that stuff from the official 8020inc company LOL you can buy 2 bucks worth of stuff from them and the shipping will be like 10 bucks HA HA

replies.text: all the zoro purchases under 50 are 5 bucks here in the USA and that above is the same 5bucks or free depending on how much you got.
